scoring_doc: Each item scored 0 / 0.5 / 1. Item score multiplied by item weight. Final score = sum(item_score * weight) / sum(weight). Pass threshold = 0.75. Items must be observable from the model's output text alone (LLM judge applies them). v1 adds 3 strict-mode items (structural-difference-not-better, flip-trigger-named, non-flagged-mode-justified) targeting structural reasoning beyond v0's specificity floor. Total weight grows from 7.5 to 10.5; pass threshold stays at 0.75 of total. Strong v0-passing analyses typically score 0.80-0.92 on v1 unless they engage structural reasoning explicitly.
judge_instructions: You are a strict but fair judge applying this rubric to a model's analysis. For each item: (1) read the test, (2) inspect the named output section, (3) score 1.0 if all conditions met, 0.5 if partially met, 0 if not met. The 'critical_application' mode means: a model that critically engages with Moore's framework on contested claims (e.g., notes that bottom-up product-led growth changes chasm dynamics) should NOT be penalized as long as the framework's core mechanics are applied rigorously. Score the application, not the agreement.

items.test: Output names exactly one TALC stage from {Innovators, Early Adopters, Early Majority, Late Majority, Laggards} AND cites at least one specific signal from the input (customer profile, sales motion, buying motivation, or specific quote) as supporting evidence.
fail_modes: Hedges across multiple stages ('between Early Adopters and Early Majority')
Names a stage with no input evidence cited
Refuses to pick a stage citing 'insufficient data' when input contains buyer-profile signals
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if a single stage is named but evidence is weak (handwaves at 'the company is growing') or if evidence is cited but the stage choice is poorly aligned with the cited evidence.

items.test: Output picks exactly one chasm position from {before the chasm, approaching the chasm, in the chasm, across the chasm} AND the reasoning explicitly references the buying motivations of current customers (visionary vs. pragmatist, or product-led/bottom-up vs. enterprise-procurement) — NOT just revenue/customer count/growth rate.
fail_modes: Justifies chasm position by citing growth alone ('revenue is up, so they're crossing')
Hedges across multiple positions
Confuses 'more visionaries adopting' with 'crossing into early majority' (this is the central Moore trap)
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if position is picked decisively but the buying-motivation reasoning is weak (mentions buyer types in passing without using them as the basis for the call).

items.test: If the output recommends a beachhead (required when company is before/in chasm), the segment is specified at role-level: a named buyer title or buyer role within a named industry/size band. Category-level segments ('SMBs', 'tech companies', 'enterprises', 'developers') do NOT pass. If the company is across the chasm, the test is whether the output names the historical beachhead with the same specificity.
fail_modes: Category-only segment ('mid-market SaaS')
Lists multiple beachheads in parallel — Moore's central rule is ONE beachhead
Specifies segment by industry without naming a buyer role or buying authority
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if buyer role is named but industry/size context is missing, or vice versa.

items.test: Output lists at least 3 concrete items the beachhead segment would refuse to buy without. 'Concrete' means: (a) integration with a named tool or standard (Okta SAML, SCIM, Salesforce, Slack), or (b) a named certification (FedRAMP, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, HIPAA), or (c) a specific feature class with name (audit log, role-based access control, data residency), or (d) a named reference customer type ('Fortune 500 manufacturers').
fail_modes: Generic items only ('better integrations', 'more references', 'stronger enterprise features', 'enterprise-ready')
Fewer than 3 concrete items
Items are aspirational rather than gap-named ('build trust' vs. 'ship SOC 2 Type II')
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if 1-2 items are concrete and the rest are generic; score 0.5 if 3+ items are listed but only 1 is genuinely named (e.g., one 'Okta SSO' surrounded by 'better admin tools', 'more references').
items.discrimination_note: This is the 1.5x-weighted item. Generic AI output ALWAYS hits this with 'better integrations.' A model that names Okta, SCIM, FedRAMP demonstrates real framework application. The score gap on this item is the single biggest signal of GTM reasoning quality.

items.test: Output lists at least 2 next-segments AND for each segment provides a one-line rationale that explicitly connects to the previous segment's reference value, shared whole-product needs, or cultural adjacency. Rationale must connect segments — not just list them.
fail_modes: Lists segments with no rationale ('then media, then enterprise')
Rationale is 'this is a big market' (TAM, not falling-rationale)
Segments are not adjacent in any meaningful sense
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if 2 segments listed with rationale but only one segment's rationale is causal (the other is filler).

items.test: For each Moore failure mode whose evidence is clearly present in the input, the output flags it AND cites or quotes the specific input signal. False positives (flagging a mode whose evidence is weak) do not penalize. False negatives on clear-evidence modes do.
fail_modes: Misses parallel pursuit when expansion thesis explicitly says 'all customers' or 'all sizes'
Misses whole product blindness when input lists missing critical infrastructure (SSO/admin/compliance)
Misses confused TALC stage when founders publicly claim early-majority readiness while customers are visionaries
Flags failure modes with no input evidence cited (still scores 0 — must cite)
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if at least one major failure mode (the one with strongest input evidence) is flagged with evidence, but other clearly-evidenced modes are missed.

items.section: Chasm Position OR Failure Modes Flagged OR TALC Stage
items.test: If the input shows revenue/customer growth among visionary buyers (early adopters), the output explicitly distinguishes whether that growth represents 'continued penetration of the visionary segment' OR 'crossing into early majority.' The distinction must be explicit in the prose — not implied. Inputs that show NO growth signal can score this 1.0 by default (N/A).
fail_modes: Treats growth as evidence of crossing without examining buyer profile of growth cohort
Implies the distinction without making it explicit
Misses entirely when input clearly shows growth among visionaries
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if the distinction is gestured at ('the growth is mostly from technical buyers') but not explicitly framed as 'visionary penetration vs. crossing.'

items.test: Output states integer confidence (1-10) for both TALC stage AND chasm position AND lists at least 2 specific facts that, if known, would change the analysis. 'Specific' means a named metric, ratio, or attribute (e.g., 'paid-tier revenue % of total', 'cohort retention past 50 members') — NOT generic ('more customer data', 'more research', 'understanding their pipeline').
fail_modes: No confidence stated
Confidence stated for only one of the two calls
Open questions are generic ('more market research', 'better data')
Fewer than 2 open questions
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if confidence stated for both but only 1 specific open question, OR confidence stated for one call only with 2+ specific open questions.

items.section: any (especially Whole Product Gap or Beachhead Recommendation)
items.test: Output names at least one structural reason existing solutions fail — different distribution model, different counterparty type, different architectural property, different identity claim — NOT a 'better/faster/cheaper' improvement claim. The structural reason must be specific enough that a buyer could verify it directly. Example pass: 'Bedrock-mediated access makes AWS the contractual counterparty; pragmatist buyers in regulated industries need the model maker as the counterparty.' Example fail: 'Our product has better features.'
fail_modes: Generic 'we're better' framing throughout the analysis
Vague differentiation: 'more flexibility,' 'higher quality,' 'faster.'
Names attributes but not reasons existing solutions cannot match them
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if a structural reason is hinted at but not explicitly named, OR if one of multiple differentiation claims is structural while others are 'better.'
items.v1_strict_note: v1 strict-mode item. Strong analyses engage structural reasoning, not superlative claims. The discrimination target: weak models default to 'better/faster' framing; strong models name structural reasons existing solutions cannot match.

items.test: At least one open question names a measurable threshold that, if observed, would FLIP the diagnosis (e.g., 'if >50% of revenue is from segment X, the call inverts'). Not just 'we'd know more' — a specific value at which the recommendation changes direction.
fail_modes: Open questions are all 'we'd benefit from more data'
Thresholds named but flip-direction not stated
Specific metrics named but no value-at-which-conclusion-changes given
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if a threshold is named but the flip-direction isn't explicit, OR if the question is concrete but doesn't name a numeric value.
items.v1_strict_note: v1 strict-mode item. Strong analyses pre-mortem their own conclusions by naming what would falsify them. The discrimination target: weak models name vague open questions; strong models name flip-conditions with specific values.

items.test: For at least 2 failure modes the analysis chose NOT to flag, output explicitly states why with reference to what would need to be present in the inputs to flag them. Demonstrates rubric discipline beyond just listing flagged modes — proves the analyst made a deliberate choice rather than missing the mode.
fail_modes: Only lists flagged modes; silent on non-flagged ones
Mentions non-flagged modes without rationale for why they're not flagged
Generic 'no other failure modes apply' without justification
items.partial_credit: Score 0.5 if 1 non-flagged mode is justified but others are silent.
items.v1_strict_note: v1 strict-mode item. Strong analyses show their work on what was deliberately not flagged. The discrimination target: weak models flag a subset and stay silent on the rest; strong models explicitly justify non-flagging.

validation.ground_truth_note: v1 raises the bar. Applied to slack-2014 ground_truth verdict, this rubric should score ≥ 0.85 (down from v0's ≥ 0.95). If the ground-truth verdict doesn't meet 0.85 on v1, either the rubric is mis-calibrated OR the ground-truth verdict didn't engage structural reasoning explicitly (which is itself diagnostic — strict-mode items reveal which historical 'good' analyses were good on specificity but weak on structural reasoning).

discrimination_targets.strong_analysis: ≥ 0.85 (down from v0's 0.90 — strict items pull strong analyses down to the 80s)

validation.pass_threshold_rationale: Pass threshold stays at 0.75 of total weight (now 7.875 absolute on 10.5 total). Strong v0-passing analyses (which scored 0.95-1.00 on v0) typically score 0.80-0.92 on v1 because they hit all v0 items but miss 1-2 strict items. Truly strong analyses that engage structural reasoning, name flip-triggers, and justify non-flagged modes hit 0.95+ on v1. The compression of strong scores to 0.85-1.00 is the discrimination band v0 lacked.
